preFix.title: Priming bare plasterboard before painting
preFix.subtitle: Apply a mist coat and primer to seal bare board
preFix.excerpt: How to seal and prime bare plasterboard before applying emulsion or specialist paint: mixing and applying a mist coat, understanding why bare board must never take full-strength emulsion on the first pass, and building up to a paintable surface.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Gypsum White Book technical reference. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

content.text: Bare plasterboard has a paper face that is porous and absorbent. Apply full-strength emulsion to it and the paint soaks in unevenly, drying blotchy and raising the paper fibres. The board then needs repainting several times more than it should. The fix is a
mist coat
applied first: emulsion thinned with water that seals the surface and gives subsequent coats something to bond to. If the board has been given a
skim coat
of plaster, the same mist-coat rule applies until the plaster is fully cured and dry.
What you will need

White matt emulsion (any brand), a 9-inch roller with a medium-pile sleeve, a 50 mm brush for cutting in, a paint tray, masking tape, and a cotton dust sheet. No specialist plasterboard primer is needed for domestic use; diluted emulsion does the same job at lower cost.
Step 1, check the board is ready

New plasterboard can be mist-coated as soon as it is fixed and jointed. Skim-coated plasterboard must be fully dry before any paint is applied: the surface changes from a dark, slightly pink colour to an even pale cream. In a warm, ventilated room this takes 2 to 4 days for a 2 to 3 mm skim. Painting too soon traps moisture and causes the surface to crack or bubble later.
Step 2, mix the mist coat

Pour emulsion into a clean bucket. Add water at a ratio of approximately 1 part water to 4 parts paint and stir thoroughly. The mixed paint should flow like single cream rather than sitting as a thick skin. Different emulsion brands absorb differently; err toward the thinner end for very raw board and lean toward the thicker end for a skim coat that is fully cured.
Step 3, apply the mist coat

Mask skirting boards and lay the dust sheet. Cut in at the top and bottom of the wall with the sash brush, then roll the rest of the surface with the 9-inch roller. Keep the strokes even; a thin, continuous coat is the aim. The coat will dry within an hour in normal conditions. Do not attempt a second coat until the first is fully dry to the touch.
Step 4, check and apply a second mist coat if needed

Once the first mist coat is dry, check the surface by running a hand across it. If the paper face still feels slightly furry or the surface is visibly patchy, apply a second mist coat at the same dilution. Two mist coats are common on raw board. If the first coat has sealed the surface smoothly, proceed directly to full-strength emulsion on the next pass.
Step 5, light sanding before the finish coat

When the mist coat is dry, run 120-grit sandpaper on a sanding block lightly over the surface. The mist coat raises the surface fibres slightly; sanding knocks them back and gives the finish coat a better surface to bond to. Wipe the dust with a slightly damp cloth, allow to dry, then apply full-strength emulsion in the usual way.
Variations

If painting with a coloured emulsion, mix the mist coat from the coloured tin; this means fewer finish coats for full coverage. For bare plasterboard in a garage or utility space, a specialist plasterboard primer can replace diluted emulsion; it seals faster.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Strip the peeling areas back to bare board, apply a mist coat, allow to dry fully, and repaint. Do not simply paint over loose paint.
attrs.cause: Full-strength emulsion applied to bare board without a mist coat; the paint bonded to the paper face rather than soaking in.
attrs.symptom: Paint peeling in sheets on plasterboard

attrs.fix: Allow the surface to dry completely, then investigate any moisture source behind the board before repainting.
attrs.cause: Skim coat was not fully dry when the mist coat was applied, or moisture was present behind the board.
attrs.symptom: Surface bubbling or blistering after painting

attrs.fix: Apply a second mist coat to even the absorption, allow to dry, then apply finish coat.
attrs.cause: Mist coat not applied evenly, leaving patches of raw board that absorb the finish coat at a different rate.
attrs.symptom: Uneven sheen on the finish coat
